This was a major blow to early AI research and is the classic demonstration of the **limits of linear classifiers** (famously highlighted by Minsky & Papert in *Perceptrons*, 1969).

**How to Address the XOR Limitation:**

1. **Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP):** Stack multiple perceptron-like units in layers. A single hidden layer with nonlinear activation (e.g., sigmoid) can solve XOR. The hidden layer learns a nonlinear transformation of the input, making the data linearly separable in the new representation.

2. **Feature Engineering / Kernel Methods:** Manually engineer nonlinear features. For XOR, the transformation $\phi(x) = [x_{1}, x_{2}, x_{1} \cdot x_{2}]^{T}$ makes the data linearly separable. Kernel SVMs automate this idea.

3. **Nonlinear Basis Functions:** Replace the linear score $w^{T}x$ with a sum of fixed nonlinear basis functions, e.g., $w^{T}\phi(x)$ where $\phi$ is a polynomial or radial basis function expansion.

4. **Deep Learning:** Modern deep neural networks are universal function approximators and can learn arbitrarily complex decision boundaries.

**Modern Significance:** The XOR problem was the historical motivation for multi-layer neural networks and the development of backpropagation (Rumelhart, Hinton, Williams, 1986), ultimately leading to today's deep learning revolution.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Examinations (Module 1 ŌĆö Linear Methods for Classification):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention in the Perceptron update.** Students often write $w \leftarrow w + \eta(\hat{y} - y)x$, which is backwards. The correct form is $w \leftarrow w + \eta(y - \hat{y})x$. This single sign error can cost 2-3 marks.
>
> 2. **Missing the conditional independence assumption in Naive Bayes.** Examiners specifically look for the statement: *"Features are independent given the class label."* A vague answer like "Naive Bayes assumes independence" will lose at least 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Not converting the product to a sum in Naive Bayes.** When explaining why we take the logarithm, students frequently say "to avoid underflow" but forget to mention it also enables efficient computation by converting products to sums. Both points are required.
>
> 4. **Confusing the sigmoid with softmax.** Logistic regression uses sigmoid for **binary** classification only. For multi-class, you need the softmax function. Examiners will deduct marks if you claim "sigmoid is used for multi-class."
>
> 5. **Skipping intermediate steps in the gradient derivation.** A full credit answer must show: $\partial \ell / \partial p$, $\partial p / \partial z$, $\partial z / \partial w$, and the cancellation of $p(1-p)$. Skipping any of these steps costs at least 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to normalize by $N$.** The loss in logistic regression is conventionally defined as the *mean* negative log-likelihood, not the *sum*. Forgetting the $\frac{1}{N}$ factor may cost 1 mark in a derivation question.
>
> 7. **Not addressing the XOR problem explicitly.** When asked about perceptron limitations, the XOR problem is the *expected* answer. A vague "cannot handle complex data" will lose marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Reference Revision Checklist:**

### Logistic Regression
- [ ] Sigmoid: $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$, output in $(0,1)$.
- [ ] Loss = **Binary Cross-Entropy** = negative log-likelihood.
- [ ] Gradient: $\nabla_{w}\ell = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i}(\sigma(w^{T}x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})x^{(i)}$.
- [ ] Loss is **convex** ŌåÆ global optimum guaranteed.
- [ ] Update: $w \leftarrow w + \eta \cdot \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i}(y^{(i)} - p^{(i)})x^{(i)}$.
- [ ] **Discriminative** model ŌĆö directly models $P(y \mid x)$.
- [ ] Use for: medical risk, credit scoring, CTR prediction, spam detection.

### Perceptron
- [ ] Decision: $\hat{y} = \text{sign}(w^{T}x + b)$, with $y \in \{-1, +1\}$.
- [ ] Update (on misclassification only): $w \leftarrow w + \eta(y - \hat{y})x$.
- [ ] **Convergence Theorem:** Finite steps on linearly separable data (Rosenblatt 1958 / Novikoff 1962).
- [ ] **Fails on XOR** ŌĆö cannot handle non-linearly-separable data.
- [ ] Solutions: multi-layer perceptron, kernel methods, deep learning.
- [ ] **Geometric** / error-correction paradigm.
- [ ] Use for: online learning, foundational NN building block, OCR.

### Naive Bayes
- [ ] Bayes' Theorem: $P(y \mid x) = \frac{P(x \mid y)P(y)}{P(x)}$.
- [ ] **Conditional independence assumption:** $P(x \mid y) = \prod_{j=1}^{d} P(x_{j} \mid y)$.
- [ ] Decision: $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c} P(c)\prod_{j=1}^{d} P(x_{j} \mid c)$.
- [ ] **Laplace smoothing** to avoid zero-frequency: $\hat{P} = (N_{c,v} + \alpha)/(N_{c} + \alpha V)$.
- [ ] **Three variants:** Gaussian (continuous), Multinomial (counts), Bernoulli (binary).
- [ ] **Generative** model ŌĆö models $P(x \mid y)$ and $P(y)$.
- [ ] Use the **log-space** trick for numerical stability.
- [ ] Use for: text classification, spam filtering, sentiment analysis, news tagging.

### Philosophical Contrast
- [ ] **Logistic Regression** = discriminative + probabilistic + convex loss.
- [ ] **Perceptron** = discriminative + non-probabilistic + perceptron criterion loss.
- [ ] **Naive Bayes** = generative + probabilistic + MLE closed-form.
- [ ] Logistic regression typically outperforms Naive Bayes when the independence assumption is badly violated and enough data is available.
- [ ] Naive Bayes is the winner when data is scarce or features are very high-dimensional (e.g., bag-of-words with $d > 10{,}000$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decision Tree Algorithm (ID3)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT632 - PECNT632
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Decision Tree Algorithm (ID3)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **ID3 (Iterative Dichotomiser 3)** algorithm is a greedy, top-down, divide-and-conquer decision tree induction algorithm introduced by **J. Ross Quinlan in 1986**. It is used for supervised classification learning and constructs a decision tree by recursively partitioning the training dataset based on the attribute that yields the **maximum Information Gain** (equivalently, the minimum weighted entropy after the split) at each internal node.

Formally, given a training set $S$ of instances belonging to $C$ classes, ID3 selects the attribute $A$ that maximizes:

$$\text{Gain}(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$$

where $H(S)$ is the Shannon entropy of the original set and $H(S \mid A)$ is the conditional entropy of $S$ given attribute $A$. The selected attribute becomes the decision (test) node, and the process is recursively applied to each partitioned subset until all instances at a node belong to a single class, or no further information gain is achievable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECNT632 Module 2):**
> ID3 is the *foundational* decision tree learner. The 2024 scheme expects students to (i) compute Entropy and Information Gain by hand for small datasets, (ii) trace the recursive tree-building process, and (iii) state the limitations that motivated successors like C4.5 and CART.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Terminology Lock-in:** The official KTU 2024 descriptor uses the exact phrases **"Information Gain," "Entropy," "Splitting Attribute,"** and **"Recursive Partitioning."** Always use these words verbatim in 14-mark derivations to align with the official marking scheme.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a doctor diagnosing a patient with symptoms like *fever, cough, throat-pain, and body-ache*. You do not ask random questions; you ask the question that **most reduces your uncertainty** about whether the illness is flu, COVID, or a common cold. You start with the *single symptom* that splits your patient population into the most homogeneous sub-groups (e.g., *high fever* ŌåÆ very likely COVID). Once a sub-group is nearly pure, you move to the next most informative symptom for the remaining uncertainty.

ID3 does exactly this with data:

- **Patient population** ŌåÆ training dataset $S$.
- **Symptoms** ŌåÆ features/attributes.
- **Most informative question first** ŌåÆ attribute with highest **Information Gain** becomes the root node.
- **Stopping when diagnosis is certain** ŌåÆ recursion halts when a node is *pure* (entropy = 0) or no attribute can reduce uncertainty further.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Viva:**
> *ID3 = "Intelligent Dichotomiser that Divides Data 3-ways" ŌĆö it always picks the attribute that maximally **D**ecreases uncertainty at **E**ach step, **D**escending the tree greedily.*

### 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics Used

| Metric | Symbol | Range | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Entropy** | $H(S)$ | $[0, 1]$ in bits for binary | Measures *disorder* / impurity of $S$ |
| **Information Gain** | $\text{Gain}(S, A)$ | $\geq 0$ | Reduction in entropy after splitting on $A$ |
| **Conditional Entropy** | $H(S \mid A)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Weighted average entropy of child partitions |
| **Base of logarithm** | $\log_2$ | ŌĆö | Information measured in **bits** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Using natural logarithm $\ln$ instead of $\log_2$ yields incorrect *bit* values. For Part B 14-mark problems, always specify that entropy is measured in **bits** and use $\log_2$ unless the question explicitly states otherwise.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Decomposed

The ID3 algorithm executes the following structured logic at every node of the growing tree:

1. **Compute Current Impurity** ŌĆö Calculate the Shannon entropy $H(S)$ of the instance set reaching the current node. If $H(S) = 0$, the node is a **leaf** assigned to the majority class; recursion stops.
2. **Enumerate Candidate Attributes** ŌĆö For every remaining feature $A$ not yet used on the current path, compute the conditional entropy $H(S \mid A)$ obtained by splitting $S$ on each distinct value of $A$.
3. **Compute Information Gain** ŌĆö Calculate $\text{Gain}(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$ for every candidate $A$.
4. **Select the Splitting Attribute** ŌĆö Choose $A^* = \arg\max_{A} \text{Gain}(S, A)$. This becomes the **test** at the current node.
5. **Branch on Each Value** ŌĆö For every value $v$ of $A^*$, create a child node containing the subset $S_v = \{x \in S : A^*(x) = v\}$.
6. **Recurse** ŌĆö Apply steps 1ŌĆō5 to each child node independently with the reduced candidate set (used attributes are *not* reused on the same path).
7. **Termination Conditions** ŌĆö Recursion stops when **any** of the following holds:
   - All instances at the node share the same class label (pure node).
   - The candidate attribute set is empty (majority-vote leaf).
   - $\text{Gain}(S, A) = 0$ for all remaining attributes (no further information gain achievable).
   - A pre-defined depth limit or minimum-sample threshold is reached (pruning heuristic).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why Entropy?** It is the only impurity measure (under mild regularity conditions) that is symmetric, non-negative, maximal at uniform distribution, and additive for independent sources ŌĆö satisfying the axioms of information theory established by Shannon (1948).
- **Why Maximize Information Gain?** Equivalent to minimizing the weighted post-split entropy, which directly reduces the expected number of further questions needed to classify an instance. This is the *greedy* heuristic.
- **Why Recurse on Subsets?** Each branch represents a more homogeneous sub-population. The recursion exploits the **decomposability** of entropy.
- **Why Greedy?** It is computationally efficient ($O(n \cdot d \cdot \log n)$ for $n$ instances and $d$ attributes) and produces interpretable trees, although it is *not guaranteed* to find the globally optimal tree (an NP-complete problem).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Shannon entropy of set $S$ with class probabilities $p_i$ | bits, $[0, \log_2 C]$ |
| 2 | $p_i = \frac{\vert S_i \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | Empirical class probability | $[0, 1]$ |
| 3 | $H(S \mid A) = \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \, H(S_v)$ | Conditional entropy after splitting on attribute $A$ | bits |
| 4 | $\text{Gain}(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$ | Information gain of attribute $A$ | bits, $\geq 0$ |
| 5 | $\text{SplitInfo}(S, A) = -\sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | Intrinsic split information (used by C4.5, **not** ID3) | bits |
| 6 | $\text{GainRatio}(S, A) = \dfrac{\text{Gain}(S, A)}{\text{SplitInfo}(S, A)}$ | Gain Ratio (C4.5 normalization) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $0 \cdot \log_2 0 \equiv 0$ | **Convention** ŌĆö required to avoid $0 \times (-\infty)$ undefined form | ŌĆö |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** ID3 uses only **Formula 4 (Information Gain)**, not Gain Ratio. If the question explicitly says *ID3*, never quote Formula 6 ŌĆö that is a C4.5 concept and will cost marks.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnosis Systems:** Symptom-based triage decision trees mirror ID3's question-selection logic.
- **Embedded / Edge ML:** Decision trees are white-box models that compile to a handful of `if-else` statements ŌĆö deployable on microcontrollers (e.g., Arduino, ESP32) for IoT fault detection.
- **Network Intrusion Detection:** ID3-style trees quickly classify packets using low-compute features (protocol, port, flag bits).
- **Credit Scoring & Risk Engineering:** Banking domain uses CART-style trees to assign probability of default to loan applicants.
- **Manufacturing Quality Control:** Sensor-streamed parameters (temperature, vibration) trigger root-cause decisions in real time.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example Used:** The classic *Play Tennis* dataset (Quinlan, 1986) ŌĆö 14 training days, 4 features, 2 classes (Yes / No). This is the standard KTU textbook example.

### 3.1 The Play Tennis Training Set

| Day | Outlook | Temperature | Humidity | Wind | Play Tennis (Class) |
|:---:|:-------:|:-----------:|:--------:|:----:|:-------------------:|
| 1  | Sunny   | Hot   | High   | Weak   | No  |
| 2  | Sunny   | Hot   | High   | Strong | No  |
| 3  | Overcast| Hot   | High   | Weak   | Yes |
| 4  | Rain    | Mild  | High   | Weak   | Yes |
| 5  | Rain    | Cool  | Normal | Weak   | Yes |
| 6  | Rain    | Cool  | Normal | Strong | No  |
| 7  | Overcast| Cool  | Normal | Strong | Yes |
| 8  | Sunny   | Mild  | High   | Weak   | No  |
| 9  | Sunny   | Cool  | Normal | Weak   | Yes |
| 10 | Rain    | Mild  | Normal | Weak   | Yes |
| 11 | Sunny   | Mild  | Normal | Strong | Yes |
| 12 | Overcast| Mild  | High   | Strong | Yes |
| 13 | Overcast| Hot   | Normal | Weak   | Yes |
| 14 | Rain    | Mild  | High   | Strong | No  |

**Class distribution:** Yes = 9, No = 5, Total = 14.

### 3.2 Step 1 ŌĆö Root Entropy $H(S)$

Apply Formula 1:

$$\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -\sum_{i=1}^{2} p_i \log_2 p_i \\
&= -\left( \frac{9}{14} \log_2 \frac{9}{14} \right) - \left( \frac{5}{14} \log_2 \frac{5}{14} \right) \\
&= -\bigl( 0.6429 \times (-0.6374) \bigr) - \bigl( 0.3571 \times (-1.4854) \bigr) \\
&= 0.4098 + 0.5303 \\
&= \mathbf{0.940 \text{ bits}}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Logic:** $\log_2(9/14) = \log_2(0.6429) \approx -0.6374$ and $\log_2(5/14) = \log_2(0.3571) \approx -1.4854$. The system begins with **0.940 bits** of uncertainty.

### 3.3 Step 2 ŌĆö Conditional Entropy for *Outlook* Attribute

The *Outlook* attribute has three values: *Sunny, Overcast, Rain*. Partition $S$:

- $S_{\text{Sunny}}$: Days {1, 2, 8, 9, 11} ŌåÆ 2 Yes, 3 No (5 total)
- $S_{\text{Overcast}}$: Days {3, 7, 12, 13} ŌåÆ 4 Yes, 0 No (4 total)
- $S_{\text{Rain}}$: Days {4, 5, 6, 10, 14} ŌåÆ 3 Yes, 2 No (5 total)

Compute sub-entropies:

$$\begin{aligned}
H(S_{\text{Sunny}}) &= -\frac{2}{5}\log_2\frac{2}{5} - \frac{3}{5}\log_2\frac{3}{5} \\
&= -\bigl(0.4 \times (-1.3219)\bigr) - \bigl(0.6 \times (-0.7370)\bigr) \\
&= 0.5288 + 0.4422 = 0.9710 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
H(S_{\text{Overcast}}) &= -\frac{4}{4}\log_2\frac{4}{4} - \frac{0}{4}\log_2\frac{0}{4} \\
&= -1 \cdot \log_2 1 - 0 = 0 \text{ bits} \quad \text{(pure node)}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
H(S_{\text{Rain}}) &= -\frac{3}{5}\log_2\frac{3}{5} - \frac{2}{5}\log_2\frac{2}{5} \\
&= 0.9710 \text{ bits} \quad \text{(by symmetry with Sunny)}
\end{aligned}$$

Apply Formula 3:

$$\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid \text{Outlook}) &= \frac{5}{14}(0.9710) + \frac{4}{14}(0) + \frac{5}{14}(0.9710) \\
&= 0.3468 + 0 + 0.3468 \\
&= \mathbf{0.6936 \text{ bits}}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.4 Step 3 ŌĆö Information Gain for *Outlook*

Apply Formula 4:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Gain}(S, \text{Outlook}) &= H(S) - H(S \mid \text{Outlook}) \\
&= 0.940 - 0.6936 \\
&= \mathbf{0.2464 \text{ bits}}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.5 Step 4 ŌĆö Repeat for Remaining Attributes

The same procedure is applied to *Temperature, Humidity, Wind*. The full analytical matrix is given below.

| Attribute $A$ | Branches $(v_i)$ | Sub-set sizes | Sub-entropy $H(S_v)$ | $H(S \mid A)$ | $\text{Gain}(S, A)$ |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Outlook** | Sunny / Overcast / Rain | 5 / 4 / 5 | 0.971 / 0.000 / 0.971 | 0.694 | **0.246 Ōśģ** |
| Temperature | Hot / Mild / Cool | 4 / 6 / 4 | 1.000 / 0.918 / 0.811 | 0.911 | 0.029 |
| Humidity | High / Normal | 7 / 7 | 0.985 / 0.592 | 0.789 | 0.151 |
| Wind | Weak / Strong | 8 / 6 | 0.811 / 1.000 | 0.892 | 0.048 |

> **Verification of *Humidity* computation:**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> H(S_{\text{High}}) &= -\frac{3}{7}\log_2\frac{3}{7} - \frac{4}{7}\log_2\frac{4}{7} = 0.985 \text{ bits}\\
> H(S_{\text{Normal}}) &= -\frac{6}{7}\log_2\frac{6}{7} - \frac{1}{7}\log_2\frac{1}{7} = 0.592 \text{ bits}\\
> H(S \mid \text{Humidity}) &= \tfrac{7}{14}(0.985) + \tfrac{7}{14}(0.592) = 0.789\\
> \text{Gain}(S, \text{Humidity}) &= 0.940 - 0.789 = 0.151 \text{ bits}
> \end{aligned}$$

### 3.6 Step 5 ŌĆö Root Selection and Branching

$$\boxed{A^* = \arg\max_A \text{Gain}(S, A) = \text{Outlook (Gain} = 0.246 \text{ bits)}}$$

**Tree after the root split:**

- *Outlook = Overcast* ŌåÆ **Leaf: Yes (4/4 pure)**, entropy = 0.
- *Outlook = Sunny* ŌåÆ Subset of 5 days ŌåÆ **Recurse**.
- *Outlook = Rain* ŌåÆ Subset of 5 days ŌåÆ **Recurse**.

### 3.7 Step 6 ŌĆö Recursion on the *Sunny* Subset (5 instances)

Subset $S_{\text{Sunny}}$ = {Day 1, 2, 8, 9, 11} with 2 Yes, 3 No.

$$H(S_{\text{Sunny}}) = 0.971 \text{ bits}$$

Remaining attributes: Temperature, Humidity, Wind.

| Attribute | Sub-entropies | $H(S_{\text{Sunny}} \mid A)$ | Gain |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Temperature | Hot(2) = 1.000; Mild(2) = 1.000; Cool(1) = 0.000 | 0.800 | 0.171 |
| Humidity | High(3) = 0.918; Normal(2) = 1.000 | 0.967 | **0.004** |
| Wind | Weak(3) = 0.918; Strong(2) = 1.000 | 0.967 | 0.004 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction to working note:** Recomputing *Humidity* on $S_{\text{Sunny}}$ (3 High ŌåÆ 1Y 2N, 2 Normal ŌåÆ 2Y 0N):
> $$\begin{aligned}
> H_{\text{High}} &= -\tfrac{1}{3}\log_2\tfrac{1}{3} - \tfrac{2}{3}\log_2\tfrac{2}{3} = 0.918\\
> H_{\text{Normal}} &= 0\\
> H(S_{\text{Sunny}} \mid \text{Humidity}) &= \tfrac{3}{5}(0.918) + \tfrac{2}{5}(0) = 0.551\\
> \text{Gain} &= 0.971 - 0.551 = \mathbf{0.420} \;\;\bigstar \text{ MAX}
> \end{aligned}$$

**Therefore the splitting attribute for the Sunny branch is Humidity.**

- *Sunny, Humidity = High* ŌåÆ Leaf: **No (3/3)**
- *Sunny, Humidity = Normal* ŌåÆ Leaf: **Yes (2/2)**

### 3.8 Step 7 ŌĆö Recursion on the *Rain* Subset (5 instances)

Subset $S_{\text{Rain}}$ = {Day 4, 5, 6, 10, 14} with 3 Yes, 2 No.

$$H(S_{\text{Rain}}) = 0.971 \text{ bits}$$

| Attribute | Sub-entropies | $H(S_{\text{Rain}} \mid A)$ | Gain |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Temperature | Mild(3) = 0.918; Cool(2) = 1.000 | 0.951 | 0.020 |
| Humidity | High(2) = 1.000; Normal(3) = 0.918 | 0.951 | 0.020 |
| Wind | Weak(3) = 0.000; Strong(2) = 1.000 | 0.400 | **0.571 Ōśģ** |

**Therefore the splitting attribute for the Rain branch is Wind.**

- *Rain, Wind = Weak* ŌåÆ Leaf: **Yes (3/3 pure)**
- *Rain, Wind = Strong* ŌåÆ Leaf: **No (2/2 pure)**

### 3.9 Final Induced Decision Tree Summary

| Node | Test | Yes Leaves | No Leaves |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Root | Outlook | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |
| Ōö£ Overcast | (leaf) | 4 | 0 |
| Ōö£ Sunny ŌåÆ | Humidity | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |
| Ōöé Ōö£ High | (leaf) | 0 | 3 |
| Ōöé Ōöö Normal | (leaf) | 2 | 0 |
| Ōöö Rain ŌåÆ | Wind | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |
| Ōö£ Weak | (leaf) | 3 | 0 |
| Ōöö Strong | (leaf) | 0 | 2 |

**Total leaves = 5; Total internal decision nodes = 3; Tree depth = 2 (root excluded).**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ID3 Algorithm ŌĆö High-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Training Set S] --> B[Compute H of S using Formula 1]
    B --> C{Is S pure or no candidates?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Create Leaf Node with majority class]
    C -- No --> E[For each remaining attribute A, compute Gain S A]
    E --> F[Select A star equals argmax Gain]
    F --> G[Create Decision Node labeled A star]
    G --> H[Partition S into subsets S v for each value v of A star]
    H --> I[For each subset S v, recurse with S v]
    I --> J{All children processed?}
    J -- No --> I
    J -- Yes --> K[Return fully induced Decision Tree]
    D --> K
```

### 4.2 Induced Decision Tree (Play Tennis ŌĆö Mermaid Tree View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA((Outlook))
    rootA -- Overcast --> leafA((Yes 4 of 4))
    rootA -- Sunny --> rootB((Humidity))
    rootA -- Rain --> rootC((Wind))
    rootB -- High --> leafB((No 3 of 3))
    rootB -- Normal --> leafC((Yes 2 of 2))
    rootC -- Weak --> leafD((Yes 3 of 3))
    rootC -- Strong --> leafE((No 2 of 2))
```

### 4.3 Information Gain Comparison Bar Chart (Schematic Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GAIN[Information Gain in Bits]
        direction LR
        B1[Outlook: 0.246]
        B2[Humidity: 0.151]
        B3[Wind: 0.048]
        B4[Temperature: 0.029]
    end
    GAIN --> SEL[Selected at Root equals Outlook]
    SEL --> ROOT((Root Node))
```

### 4.4 Recursive Partitioning Schematic (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DS[(Dataset S: 14 instances, H equals 0.940)]
    DS --> SP[Split on Outlook]
    SP --> S1[Sunny: 5 inst, H equals 0.971]
    SP --> S2[Overcast: 4 inst, H equals 0.000 LEAF YES]
    SP --> S3[Rain: 5 inst, H equals 0.971]
    S1 --> SP1[Split on Humidity]
    S3 --> SP2[Split on Wind]
    SP1 --> L1[High: 3 inst, H equals 0 LEAF NO]
    SP1 --> L2[Normal: 2 inst, H equals 0 LEAF YES]
    SP2 --> L3[Weak: 3 inst, H equals 0 LEAF YES]
    SP2 --> L4[Strong: 2 inst, H equals 0 LEAF NO]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Notes:** All node IDs are alphanumeric prefixes (`rootA`, `leafA`, `SP1`, etc.) to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts. Labels use plain uppercase text only ŌĆö no markdown bold or italics inside the quoted strings.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1.** Define *Entropy* in the context of the ID3 algorithm. What is the entropy of a dataset that is perfectly homogeneous (all instances belong to a single class)?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Entropy, $H(S)$, is a measure of the *impurity* or *randomness* of a dataset $S$, defined as
$$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \log_2 p_i,$$
where $p_i$ is the proportion of instances belonging to class $i$ and $C$ is the total number of classes. For a perfectly homogeneous dataset, one $p_i = 1$ and all others are $0$, so $H(S) = -1 \cdot \log_2 1 - 0 = 0$ bits. **(3 marks: 1 ŌĆö definition, 1 ŌĆö formula, 1 ŌĆö application)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between *Information Gain* and *Gain Ratio*. State one advantage of using Gain Ratio over Information Gain.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Information Gain* is the reduction in entropy achieved by splitting on attribute $A$:
$$\text{Gain}(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A).$$
*Gain Ratio* normalizes Information Gain by the *intrinsic split information*:
$$\text{GainRatio}(S, A) = \dfrac{\text{Gain}(S, A)}{\text{SplitInfo}(S, A)}, \quad \text{SplitInfo}(S, A) = -\sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}.$$
**Advantage:** Gain Ratio penalizes attributes with many distinct values (e.g., *Date*, *ID*), which ID3's raw Information Gain would unfairly favour. Hence Gain Ratio (used in C4.5) avoids the *multi-valued attribute bias* of ID3. **(3 marks: 1 ŌĆö IG, 1 ŌĆö GR, 1 ŌĆö advantage)**

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer (ESE Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō End Semester, Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Play Tennis Full Trace**

**(a)** *[7 marks]* Given the *Play Tennis* training set of 14 instances (refer Section 3.1 of these notes), compute the **Entropy of the entire dataset** and the **Information Gain for each of the four attributes** (Outlook, Temperature, Humidity, Wind). Identify the attribute selected as the **root** of the ID3 decision tree and justify your choice.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**
The complete step-by-step derivation is presented in Sections 3.2 ŌĆō 3.5 of these notes. The expected answer board is:

| Quantity | Value (bits) |
|:---|:---:|
| $H(S)$ | **0.940** |
| Gain(Outlook) | **0.246** Ōśģ Root |
| Gain(Temperature) | 0.029 |
| Gain(Humidity) | 0.151 |
| Gain(Wind) | 0.048 |

**Justification (1 mark):** The root is *Outlook* because it maximizes Information Gain ($0.246$ bits), implying it most reduces the entropy of the dataset after splitting.

**(b)** *[7 marks]* Continuing the ID3 induction on the *Sunny* and *Rain* branches of the root, determine the **splitting attribute at each branch**, compute the corresponding **Information Gain**, and draw the **final induced decision tree**.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**
- **Sunny branch (5 instances, 2Y 3N, $H = 0.971$):** Recomputing gains (Section 3.7) gives Gain(Humidity) = **0.420 bits** as the maximum. Splits: *High ŌåÆ No*, *Normal ŌåÆ Yes*.
- **Rain branch (5 instances, 3Y 2N, $H = 0.971$):** Recomputing gains (Section 3.8) gives Gain(Wind) = **0.571 bits** as the maximum. Splits: *Weak ŌåÆ Yes*, *Strong ŌåÆ No*.
- **Overcast branch:** Pure (4Y 0N), terminates as leaf.
- **Final Tree:** 3 internal nodes, 5 leaves ŌĆö drawn as in Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram above. **(Full marks require the Mermaid-style tree or its equivalent ASCII drawing; textual description alone gets 5/7.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Numerical Problem**

**(a)** *[7 marks]* A marketing dataset contains 100 customer records, of which 60 responded positively to a campaign and 40 negatively. Compute the **entropy** of this dataset. If the *Age Group* attribute partitions the data into three groups: *Young* (20 records ŌåÆ 5Y, 15N), *Middle* (50 records ŌåÆ 35Y, 15N), and *Senior* (30 records ŌåÆ 20Y, 10N), compute the **Information Gain** for *Age Group*.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -\frac{60}{100}\log_2\frac{60}{100} - \frac{40}{100}\log_2\frac{40}{100}\\
&= -(0.6)(-0.7370) - (0.4)(-1.3219)\\
&= 0.4422 + 0.5288 = \mathbf{0.971 \text{ bits}}
\end{aligned}$$

Sub-entropies:

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{Young}} &= -\tfrac{5}{20}\log_2\tfrac{5}{20} - \tfrac{15}{20}\log_2\tfrac{15}{20}\\
&= -(0.25)(-2) - (0.75)(-0.4150)\\
&= 0.5 + 0.3113 = 0.8113 \text{ bits}\\[4pt]
H_{\text{Middle}} &= -\tfrac{35}{50}\log_2\tfrac{35}{50} - \tfrac{15}{50}\log_2\tfrac{15}{50}\\
&= -(0.7)(-0.5146) - (0.3)(-1.7370)\\
&= 0.3602 + 0.5211 = 0.8813 \text{ bits}\\[4pt]
H_{\text{Senior}} &= -\tfrac{20}{30}\log_2\tfrac{20}{30} - \tfrac{10}{30}\log_2\tfrac{10}{30}\\
&= -(0.6667)(-0.5850) - (0.3333)(-1.5850)\\
&= 0.3900 + 0.5283 = 0.9183 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

Conditional entropy:

$$\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid \text{Age}) &= \tfrac{20}{100}(0.8113) + \tfrac{50}{100}(0.8813) + \tfrac{30}{100}(0.9183)\\
&= 0.1623 + 0.4407 + 0.2755 = \mathbf{0.8785 \text{ bits}}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\text{Gain}(S, \text{Age}) = 0.971 - 0.8785 = \mathbf{0.0925 \text{ bits}}$$

**(b)** *[7 marks]* If the *Income* attribute partitions the data into two equal halves ŌĆö *High* (50 records: 40Y, 10N) and *Low* (50 records: 20Y, 30N) ŌĆö compute the **Information Gain** for *Income*. **Which attribute (Age or Income) would ID3 select as the root**, and why? State **two limitations of ID3** as identified in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{High}} &= -\tfrac{40}{50}\log_2\tfrac{40}{50} - \tfrac{10}{50}\log_2\tfrac{10}{50}\\
&= 0.4 \times 0.5288 + 0.2 \times 0.4644 = \text{wait, recompute correctly:}\\
&= -(0.8)(-0.3219) - (0.2)(-2.3219)\\
&= 0.2575 + 0.4644 = 0.7219 \text{ bits}\\[4pt]
H_{\text{Low}} &= -\tfrac{20}{50}\log_2\tfrac{20}{50} - \tfrac{30}{50}\log_2\tfrac{30}{50}\\
&= -(0.4)(-1.3219) - (0.6)(-0.7370)\\
&= 0.5288 + 0.4422 = 0.9710 \text{ bits}\\[4pt]
H(S \mid \text{Income}) &= \tfrac{50}{100}(0.7219) + \tfrac{50}{100}(0.9710)\\
&= 0.3610 + 0.4855 = \mathbf{0.8465 \text{ bits}}\\[4pt]
\text{Gain}(S, \text{Income}) &= 0.971 - 0.8465 = \mathbf{0.1245 \text{ bits}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comparison:** $\text{Gain}(\text{Income}) = 0.1245 > \text{Gain}(\text{Age}) = 0.0925$. Therefore **ID3 selects *Income* as the root attribute** because it yields the higher information gain. **(3 marks for correct comparison + 1 mark for conclusion.)**

**Two Limitations of ID3 (2 ├Ś 1.5 marks):**
1. **Multi-valued attribute bias:** Information Gain favours attributes with many distinct values (e.g., *Date*, *Customer-ID*), which can lead to overfitting and shallow, uninformative trees.
2. **No handling of continuous attributes:** ID3 requires discrete attributes; continuous features must be pre-discretized (binning) before the algorithm can use them.
3. *(Additional)* **No pruning mechanism:** ID3 grows the tree until pure leaves, making it prone to overfitting on noisy data. Successor algorithms (C4.5, CART) incorporate post-pruning.
4. *(Additional)* **Greedy, locally optimal:** ID3 does not backtrack; a globally optimal tree is not guaranteed.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks on ID3 Questions (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting the convention $0 \log_2 0 = 0$** ŌĆö when a sub-set is pure, the missing-class term is $0 \cdot \log_2 0$. Omitting this convention explicitly in the answer sheet results in a $-1$ deduction.
> 2. **Using $\ln$ instead of $\log_2$** ŌĆö entropy values will be in *nats* instead of *bits*. Examiners deduct **2 marks** if the unit is not specified correctly.
> 3. **Forgetting to weight by $\vert S_v \vert / \vert S \vert$** ŌĆö students often compute the unweighted average of sub-entropies, which is mathematically wrong. Watch for the $\frac{5}{14}, \frac{4}{14}, \frac{5}{14}$ multipliers in conditional entropy.
> 4. **Confusing ID3 (Information Gain) with C4.5 (Gain Ratio)** ŌĆö KTU may test this distinction. Always re-read whether the question specifies the algorithm.
> 5. **Not stating the splitting attribute clearly** ŌĆö the conclusion "Outlook is selected because it has the maximum gain" must include the *numerical value* (0.246 bits) to earn full marks.
> 6. **Drawing the tree without labels** ŌĆö leaf nodes must be labelled with the *class* and ideally the *count* (e.g., "Yes 3/3"). An unlabelled tree gets only partial credit.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- ŌśÉ **ID3** = Iterative Dichotomiser 3, by **Ross Quinlan (1986)**, a *greedy*, *top-down*, *divide-and-conquer* classifier.
- ŌśÉ **Entropy** $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ ŌĆö measures impurity; 0 = pure, 1 (binary) = maximum disorder.
- ŌśÉ **Information Gain** = reduction in entropy after splitting; **higher = better split**.
- ŌśÉ **Root = argmax Gain** ŌĆö selected by comparing all candidate attributes.
- ŌśÉ **Recursion stops** when node is pure, no candidates remain, or gain = 0 for all remaining attributes.
- ŌśÉ **Logarithm base 2** ŌåÆ result in *bits*; convention $0 \log_2 0 \equiv 0$.
- ŌśÉ **Conditional entropy** is a *weighted* average of sub-entropies (weights = partition sizes).
- ŌśÉ **ID3 uses ONLY Information Gain**, not Gain Ratio (that's C4.5).
- ŌśÉ **Limitations:** (1) multi-valued bias, (2) no continuous-attribute support, (3) no pruning ŌåÆ overfit, (4) greedy = no backtracking.
- ŌśÉ **Successors:** C4.5 (Quinlan, 1993) ŌĆö Gain Ratio + pruning + continuous support; CART (Breiman, 1984) ŌĆö Gini index + binary splits + regression trees.
- ŌśÉ **For KTU derivations:** always show the formula, the substitution, the sub-entropies, the weighted sum, and the final gain ŌĆö every step earns marks.
- ŌśÉ **Drawing a tree** is mandatory for 14-mark questions: use circles/boxes for decision nodes, triangles/leaves for terminal nodes, and label each edge with the attribute value.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regression - Linear Regression with One Variable
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT632 - PECNT632
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Regression - Linear Regression with One Variable

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Linear Regression with One Variable** (also called **Univariate Linear Regression** or **Simple Linear Regression**) is a *supervised learning* algorithm used in machine learning and statistical modeling that establishes a **linear relationship** between a **single independent (input) variable** $x$ and a **continuous dependent (output) variable** $y$. The objective is to learn the optimal values of model parameters $(\theta_0, \theta_1)$ that minimize a *cost function*, typically the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**, over a given training dataset.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the model is a member of the *parametric* family of **linear basis-function models**, where the prediction is computed as a linear combination of the input feature(s) and the learned parameters. It is the foundational regression model from which *multivariate*, *polynomial*, and *regularized* (Ridge/Lasso) variants are derived.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **The "House Price" Analogy:** Imagine you are a real-estate analyst. You have data about houses: for each house, you know its size in square feet (the input $x$) and its selling price in lakhs of Ōé╣ (the output $y$). When you plot the data on a graph, the points roughly form a "rising cloud." A straight line passing through the middle of this cloud ŌĆö not too steep, not too flat ŌĆö gives a reasonable *best-fit* line. Given any new house's size, you simply "drop a vertical line" to the best-fit line to predict its price. **Linear regression finds that best-fit line for you automatically!**

The mathematical shorthand is simple. We are essentially looking for a line:

$$\hat{y} = h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$$

Here, $\theta_0$ is the **y-intercept** (the predicted value when $x = 0$, e.g., base price of a plot of land with zero area) and $\theta_1$ is the **slope** (e.g., price increase per additional square foot).

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Notation (KTU Standard)

| Symbol | Meaning | Example Value (House Data) |
|---|---|---|
| $m$ | Number of training examples | $m = 5$ houses |
| $x^{(i)}$ | Input (feature) of the $i$-th example | $x^{(3)} = 1500$ sq.ft |
| $y^{(i)}$ | Output (target) of the $i$-th example | $y^{(3)} = 45$ lakhs |
| $(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ | A single training example | $(1500, 45)$ |
| $h_\theta(x)$ | Hypothesis function (model prediction) | $h_\theta(1500) = 46$ lakhs |
| $\theta_0, \theta_1$ | Model parameters to be learned | $\theta_0 = 10, \theta_1 = 0.024$ |
| $J(\theta_0, \theta_1)$ | Cost function (loss) | $J = 2.4$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of the Linear Regression Pipeline

The linear regression problem in KTU 2024 modules is decomposed into three logically distinct sub-problems:

### (a) The Hypothesis Representation
The hypothesis defines the family of functions we are willing to consider. For one variable:

$$h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$$

- **Why linear?** Because $h_\theta(x)$ is a *linear function* of $x$ and a *linear function* of the parameters $\theta_0, \theta_1$.
- **Geometric meaning:** A straight line in 2-D space $(x, y)$.

### (b) The Cost Function (Mean Squared Error)
To quantify "how good" a particular choice of $(\theta_0, \theta_1)$ is, we measure the average squared difference between predictions $h_\theta(x^{(i)})$ and actual values $y^{(i)}$:

$$J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why divide by $2m$ instead of $m$?**
> The factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ is a mathematical convenience ŌĆö it cancels out neatly when we take the partial derivative of $J$ during gradient descent (you will see this in the derivation). The cost $J$ is also called the **Squared Error Function** or **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** when expressed without the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor.

The cost function $J(\theta_0, \theta_1)$ is a **convex (bowl-shaped)** function for linear regression, which guarantees that gradient descent will converge to the *global minimum* (the single best fit) ŌĆö provided $\alpha$ is chosen appropriately.

### (c) The Optimization Algorithm (Gradient Descent)
Gradient descent is an iterative optimization procedure that tweaks $\theta_0$ and $\theta_1$ in the direction of *steepest descent* on the cost surface $J$:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} J(\theta_0, \theta_1) \quad \text{for } j = 0, 1$$

where $\alpha > 0$ is the **learning rate** (a hyperparameter).

## 2.2 The Role of the Learning Rate $\alpha$

| Condition on $\alpha$ | Behaviour |
|---|---|
| $\alpha$ is **too small** | Convergence is reliable but very *slow*; many iterations needed |
| $\alpha$ is **too large** | Updates overshoot the minimum; cost may *diverge* or oscillate wildly |
| $\alpha$ is **just right** | Cost decreases smoothly and converges in a few hundred to few thousand iterations |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Students often forget that **gradient descent must perform *simultaneous* updates** for $\theta_0$ and $\theta_1$. That is, compute the right-hand side using the *old* values of both parameters, *then* assign them together. Sequential updates lead to a buggy implementation that converges to wrong parameters.

## 2.3 Convergence & Stopping Criteria

The training is terminated when one of the following is satisfied:
1. $J(\theta_0, \theta_1)$ falls below a tolerance $\varepsilon$ (e.g., $10^{-6}$).
2. $\vert \Delta J \vert < \varepsilon$ between consecutive iterations.
3. A maximum number of iterations (e.g., $1000$) is reached.
4. The gradient norm $\sqrt{(\partial J / \partial \theta_0)^2 + (\partial J / \partial \theta_1)^2} < \varepsilon$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is your one-stop exam reference for this topic. Memorize these equations and their boundary conditions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hypothesis | $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$ | $x \in \mathbb{R}$, $h_\theta \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 2 | Cost Function (MSE) | $J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$ | $J \geq 0$; $J = 0$ at perfect fit |
| 3 | Gradient (w.r.t. $\theta_0$) | $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)$ | Always well-defined |
| 4 | Gradient (w.r.t. $\theta_1$) | $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}$ | Requires feature scaling for stable convergence |
| 5 | Update Rule ($\theta_0$) | $\theta_0 := \theta_0 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)$ | Apply **simultaneously** |
| 6 | Update Rule ($\theta_1$) | $\theta_1 := \theta_1 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}$ | Apply **simultaneously** |
| 7 | Closed-form (Normal Eqn.) | $\theta_1 = \frac{\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})}{\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^2}$, $\theta_0 = \bar{y} - \theta_1 \bar{x}$ | Works only for $n \leq \sim 10^4$ features |
| 8 | Matrix Form (Normal Eqn.) | $\boldsymbol{\theta} = (X^{T} X)^{-1} X^{T} \mathbf{y}$ | Requires $(X^{T}X)$ to be invertible |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Linear regression with one variable is the *workhorse* of countless engineering and business problems:
- **Civil Engineering:** Estimating concrete strength from curing time.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Modeling I-V characteristics of a resistor (Ohm's Law).
- **Economics:** Demand forecasting (price $\to$ quantity demanded).
- **Biomedical:** Predicting blood pressure from age.
- **Manufacturing:** Predicting tool wear from operating hours.

It also serves as a **conceptual building block** for understanding more advanced techniques such as multivariate regression, neural networks (where a single neuron with linear activation is exactly linear regression), and reinforcement learning's policy evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cost Function

**Goal:** Understand *why* we choose the squared error.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the error for a single example:**

$$e^{(i)} = h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö A naive cost would be the sum of errors**, but $\sum e^{(i)}$ can be zero even for a poor model (positive and negative errors cancel). To avoid this cancellation, we take the *absolute value* or *square* the errors. The squared form is preferred because it is **differentiable everywhere** and **penalizes large errors more heavily**.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Square and sum across all examples:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Average over $m$ and multiply by $\frac{1}{2}$** to obtain a *mean* and a *convenient derivative*:

$$J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

> The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ ensures that the $\frac{1}{m}$ in front cancels the factor of $2$ that arises when differentiating $u^2$ (chain rule: $\frac{d}{du}(u^2) = 2u$).

## 3.2 Derivation of the Gradient Descent Update Rules

**Starting point:** The general gradient descent rule is

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} J(\theta_0, \theta_1)$$

We need to compute $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0}$ and $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1}$.

### (A) Partial Derivative w.r.t. $\theta_0$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Substitute $J$ and pull the constant $\frac{1}{2m}$ out:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_0} \left[ \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2 \right] = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_0} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the chain rule** with $u = h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}$ and $\frac{du}{d\theta_0} = \frac{\partial h_\theta}{\partial \theta_0} = 1$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} 2 \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot 1$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö The factor of $2$ cancels with $\frac{1}{2}$:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute into the update rule:**

$$\theta_0 := \theta_0 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)$$

### (B) Partial Derivative w.r.t. $\theta_1$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Same setup, but now $\frac{\partial h_\theta}{\partial \theta_1} = x^{(i)}$:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} 2 \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial h_\theta(x^{(i)})}{\partial \theta_1}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Since $h_\theta(x^{(i)}) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)}$, we have $\frac{\partial h_\theta(x^{(i)})}{\partial \theta_1} = x^{(i)}$:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} 2 \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Simplify:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Update rule for $\theta_1$:**

$$\theta_1 := \theta_1 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simultaneous Update Convention:** In the KTU answer script, present the two update rules *together* and explicitly state: *"The right-hand side values must be computed using the old values of $\theta_0$ and $\theta_1$, after which both are updated at the end of the iteration."*

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem Setup:** Given the dataset $\{(1, 2),\ (2, 4),\ (3, 5)\}$ with $m = 3$ examples, find the best-fit line $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$ using **one iteration** of batch gradient descent starting from $\theta_0 = 0, \theta_1 = 0$ with learning rate $\alpha = 0.01$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $h_\theta(x^{(i)})$ at $\theta_0 = \theta_1 = 0$:**

$$h_\theta(x^{(1)}) = 0 + 0 \cdot 1 = 0, \quad h_\theta(x^{(2)}) = 0, \quad h_\theta(x^{(3)}) = 0$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the error terms $(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})$:**

$$0 - 2 = -2, \quad 0 - 4 = -4, \quad 0 - 5 = -5$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Sum of errors for the $\theta_0$ update:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{3} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) = (-2) + (-4) + (-5) = -11$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Update $\theta_0$:**

$$\theta_0 := 0 - 0.01 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot (-11) = 0 - 0.01 \cdot (-3.667) = 0.03667$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute the weighted sum for the $\theta_1$ update:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{3} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) \cdot x^{(i)} = (-2)(1) + (-4)(2) + (-5)(3) = -2 - 8 - 15 = -25$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Update $\theta_1$:**

$$\theta_1 := 0 - 0.01 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot (-25) = 0 - 0.01 \cdot (-8.333) = 0.08333$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Updated hypothesis after one iteration:**

$$h_\theta(x) = 0.03667 + 0.08333\, x$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the parameters moved *away* from zero because all errors were negative, meaning predictions were too low. The model has taken a small step toward the data. Many more iterations would be required to converge to the optimal $\theta_0 \approx 0.667$ and $\theta_1 \approx 1.5$ (which we can verify by the closed-form solution).

## 3.4 Verification via the Closed-Form (Normal) Equation

For the same dataset $\{(1,2),(2,4),(3,5)\}$:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute means:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1+2+3}{3} = 2, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{2+4+5}{3} = \frac{11}{3}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the numerator $\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})$:**

$$(1-2)(2-\tfrac{11}{3}) + (2-2)(4-\tfrac{11}{3}) + (3-2)(5-\tfrac{11}{3}) = (-1)(-\tfrac{5}{3}) + 0 + (1)(\tfrac{4}{3}) = \tfrac{5}{3} + \tfrac{4}{3} = 3$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the denominator $\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^2$:**

$$(1-2)^2 + (2-2)^2 + (3-2)^2 = 1 + 0 + 1 = 2$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $\theta_1$:**

$$\theta_1 = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute $\theta_0$:**

$$\theta_0 = \bar{y} - \theta_1 \bar{x} = \frac{11}{3} - 1.5 \cdot 2 = \frac{11}{3} - 3 = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Final hypothesis:**

$$h_\theta(x) = 0.667 + 1.5\, x$$

> This is the *unique* globally optimal line. Any gradient-descent run (with $\alpha$ small enough) will asymptotically approach these values.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Linear Regression Training Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Collect Training Data X and y] --> B[Initialize Parameters theta0 theta1 to 0 or small random]
    B --> C[Compute Hypothesis h_theta_x = theta0 + theta1 x]
    C --> D[Calculate Cost J = 1 over 2m times sum of squared errors]
    D --> E[Compute Partial Derivatives del J by del theta0 and del J by del theta1]
    E --> F[Simultaneously Update theta0 and theta1 using learning rate alpha]
    F --> G{Check Convergence or Max Iterations}
    G -->|No not converged| C
    G -->|Yes converged| H[Output Final Hypothesis h_theta_x]
    H --> I[Use Model for Predictions on New Data]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Cost Function as a 2-D Optimization Surface

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input Training Examples] --> B[Hypothesis Family h_theta_x]
    B --> C[Cost Function J as a Function of theta0 and theta1]
    C --> D[Gradient Descent Iterative Search on the Cost Surface]
    D --> E[Optimal Parameters theta0 star and theta1 star]
    E --> F[Final Best Fit Line]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style C fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style D fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style E fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style F fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828
```

## 4.3 Flowchart: Gradient Descent Algorithm (Step-by-Step Execution View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Step 0: Initialize theta0 theta1 and set learning rate alpha] --> S1[Step 1: For i = 1 to m compute h_theta of x_i]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2: Compute error = h_theta_x_i minus y_i for all examples]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Sum the errors to form grad0]
    S2 --> S4[Step 4: Multiply each error by x_i and sum to form grad1]
    S3 --> S5[Step 5a: temp0 = theta0 minus alpha times 1 over m times grad0]
    S4 --> S6[Step 5b: temp1 = theta1 minus alpha times 1 over m times grad1]
    S5 --> S7[Step 6: Simultaneously assign theta0 = temp0 and theta1 = temp1]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[Step 7: Compute new cost J and check if it has converged]
    S8 --> S9{Is change in J less than epsilon or max iters reached?}
    S9 -->|No| S1
    S9 -->|Yes| END[Return theta0 and theta1 as Final Parameters]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Schematic: Effect of Learning Rate on Convergence

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Large alpha: cost oscillates or diverges] --> X[Convergence Behaviour]
    B[Small alpha: cost decreases slowly but steadily] --> X
    C[Adequate alpha: cost decreases smoothly to minimum] --> X
    D[Optimal alpha: fast convergence to global minimum] --> X

    style A fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style D fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the hypothesis and the cost function used in linear regression with one variable. Why is the cost function divided by $2m$?
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **hypothesis** is the linear function $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$ that maps input $x$ to predicted output. *(1 mark)*
- The **cost function** measures the average squared error: $J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$. *(1 mark)*
- The factor $\frac{1}{2m}$ averages the cost over $m$ examples, and the $\frac{1}{2}$ cancels the factor of $2$ produced by the chain rule during differentiation, simplifying gradient computation. *(1 mark)*

---

### Q2. What is the role of the learning rate $\alpha$ in gradient descent? What happens if it is too small or too large?
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The learning rate $\alpha$ controls the **step size** of each parameter update in gradient descent: $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$. *(1 mark)*
- If $\alpha$ is **too small**, gradient descent converges *very slowly*, requiring many iterations to reach the minimum. *(1 mark)*
- If $\alpha$ is **too large**, the updates may *overshoot* the minimum, causing the cost to oscillate or even *diverge* to infinity. *(1 mark)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Simultaneous Updates:** Examiners *will* deduct 1ŌĆō2 marks if you write sequential updates (e.g., updating $\theta_0$ first, then plugging its new value into the $\theta_1$ update). Always emphasize *simultaneous* updates.
> 2. **Index Notation:** Sloppy use of $\sum$ (e.g., forgetting $i=1$ to $m$, or omitting the factor $\frac{1}{m}$) costs marks. Show the full expression.
> 3. **Derivative Chain Rule:** Many students forget to apply the chain rule to $(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$. Show the chain rule *explicitly* in derivations.
> 4. **Final Units / Numerical Verification:** Always substitute final numerical values; do not leave the answer in symbolic form for numerical problems.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Derive the gradient descent update rules for the parameters $\theta_0$ and $\theta_1$ in linear regression with one variable. **(7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö State the hypothesis and cost function:** *(1 mark)*

$$h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$$

$$J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Write the generic gradient descent rule:** *(1 mark)*

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} J(\theta_0, \theta_1)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Derive $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0}$:** *(2 marks)*

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} 2 \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial h_\theta(x^{(i)})}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Derive $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1}$:** *(2 marks)*

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} 2 \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Write the simultaneous update rules:** *(1 mark)*

$$\theta_0 := \theta_0 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)$$

$$\theta_1 := \theta_1 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}$$

---

#### (b) For the dataset $\{(1, 1), (2, 3), (3, 3), (4, 5)\}$, perform **two iterations** of batch gradient descent with $\alpha = 0.05$ and initial values $\theta_0 = 0, \theta_1 = 0$. Compute the cost after each iteration. **(7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify $m = 4$ and list data:** *(1 mark)*
$x^{(i)} = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$, $y^{(i)} = \{1, 3, 3, 5\}$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Iteration 1: At $\theta_0 = 0, \theta_1 = 0$, $h_\theta(x^{(i)}) = 0$ for all $i$:** *(1 mark)*

Sum of errors: $\sum (h - y) = (0-1) + (0-3) + (0-3) + (0-5) = -12$

Weighted sum: $\sum (h - y) x = (-1)(1) + (-3)(2) + (-3)(3) + (-5)(4) = -1 - 6 - 9 - 20 = -36$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Update parameters:** *(1 mark)*

$$\theta_0 := 0 - 0.05 \cdot \frac{1}{4} \cdot (-12) = 0 - 0.05 \cdot (-3) = 0.15$$

$$\theta_1 := 0 - 0.05 \cdot \frac{1}{4} \cdot (-36) = 0 - 0.05 \cdot (-9) = 0.45$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute cost after iteration 1:** *(1 mark)*

$h(1) = 0.6,\ h(2) = 1.05,\ h(3) = 1.5,\ h(4) = 1.95$

$$J = \frac{1}{2 \cdot 4} \left[ (0.6-1)^2 + (1.05-3)^2 + (1.5-3)^2 + (1.95-5)^2 \right] = \frac{1}{8} [0.16 + 3.8025 + 2.25 + 9.3025] = \frac{15.515}{8} = 1.939$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Iteration 2: Use new $\theta_0 = 0.15, \theta_1 = 0.45$:** *(1 mark)*

$h(1) = 0.6,\ h(2) = 1.05,\ h(3) = 1.5,\ h(4) = 1.95$ (same as before; the residuals are identical to iteration 1's predictions)

Sum of errors: $-12$ (same)
Weighted sum: $-36$ (same)

**Step 6 ŌĆö Update parameters (2nd time):** *(1 mark)*

$$\theta_0 := 0.15 - 0.05 \cdot \frac{1}{4} \cdot (-12) = 0.15 + 0.15 = 0.30$$

$$\theta_1 := 0.45 - 0.05 \cdot \frac{1}{4} \cdot (-36) = 0.45 + 0.45 = 0.90$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Cost after iteration 2 (new predictions):** *(1 mark)*

$h(1) = 1.2,\ h(2) = 2.1,\ h(3) = 3.0,\ h(4) = 3.9$

$$J = \frac{1}{8} [(1.2-1)^2 + (2.1-3)^2 + (3.0-3)^2 + (3.9-5)^2] = \frac{1}{8} [0.04 + 0.81 + 0 + 1.21] = \frac{2.06}{8} = 0.2575$$

**Conclusion:** The cost has dropped from $1.939$ to $0.2575$, indicating the model is learning.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice)

#### (a) Explain the closed-form (Normal) equation for linear regression. Derive the expressions for $\theta_0$ and $\theta_1$ in terms of the training data means. **(7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Motivation:** *(1 mark)*
Gradient descent iteratively approaches the optimum. For *small* numbers of features, a **direct closed-form** solution exists, obtained by setting the gradient of $J$ to zero.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Set $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = 0$ and $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = 0$:** *(1 mark)*

$$\frac{1}{m} \sum (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum (\theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) = 0$$

$$\frac{1}{m} \sum (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) x^{(i)} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum (\theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) x^{(i)} = 0$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Simplify using means** $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{m}\sum x^{(i)}$ and $\bar{y} = \frac{1}{m}\sum y^{(i)}$: *(2 marks)*

First equation gives: $m\theta_0 + \theta_1 \sum x^{(i)} = \sum y^{(i)} \Rightarrow \theta_0 + \theta_1 \bar{x} = \bar{y} \Rightarrow \theta_0 = \bar{y} - \theta_1 \bar{x}$

Second equation gives: $\theta_0 \sum x^{(i)} + \theta_1 \sum (x^{(i)})^2 = \sum x^{(i)} y^{(i)}$

Substituting $\theta_0$:

$$(\bar{y} - \theta_1 \bar{x}) \sum x^{(i)} + \theta_1 \sum (x^{(i)})^2 = \sum x^{(i)} y^{(i)}$$

$$\bar{y} \sum x^{(i)} - \theta_1 \bar{x} \sum x^{(i)} + \theta_1 \sum (x^{(i)})^2 = \sum x^{(i)} y^{(i)}$$

Dividing the $\sum x^{(i)}$ terms by $m$ (i.e., using $\bar{x}$):

$$\theta_1 \left( \sum (x^{(i)})^2 - m \bar{x}^2 \right) = \sum x^{(i)} y^{(i)} - m \bar{x} \bar{y}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Recognize the identity** $\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^2 = \sum (x^{(i)})^2 - m\bar{x}^2$ and similarly for the cross term: *(1 mark)*

$$\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})(y^{(i)} - \bar{y}) = \sum x^{(i)} y^{(i)} - m \bar{x} \bar{y}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Final closed-form expressions:** *(1 mark)*

$$\boxed{\theta_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^2}}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_0 = \bar{y} - \theta_1 \bar{x}}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Matrix form** (bonus 1 mark):

$$\boldsymbol{\theta} = (X^{T} X)^{-1} X^{T} \mathbf{y}$$

---

#### (b) For the dataset $\{(1, 2), (2, 3), (3, 5), (4, 4), (5, 6)\}$, find the best-fit line using the closed-form (Normal) equation. **(7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2021]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö List data and compute means:** *(1 mark)*

$\bar{x} = \frac{1+2+3+4+5}{5} = 3$, $\bar{y} = \frac{2+3+5+4+6}{5} = 4$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the deviations table:** *(1 mark)*

| $i$ | $x^{(i)}$ | $y^{(i)}$ | $x^{(i)} - \bar{x}$ | $y^{(i)} - \bar{y}$ | Product | $(x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 2 | $-2$ | $-2$ | $4$ | $4$ |
| 2 | 2 | 3 | $-1$ | $-1$ | $1$ | $1$ |
| 3 | 3 | 5 | $0$ | $1$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| 4 | 4 | 4 | $1$ | $0$ | $0$ | $1$ |
| 5 | 5 | 6 | $2$ | $2$ | $4$ | $4$ |
| **Sum** |  |  |  |  | **$9$** | **$10$** |

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $\theta_1$:** *(1 mark)*

$$\theta_1 = \frac{9}{10} = 0.9$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $\theta_0$:** *(1 mark)*

$$\theta_0 = 4 - 0.9 \cdot 3 = 4 - 2.7 = 1.3$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Final best-fit line:** *(1 mark)*

$$h_\theta(x) = 1.3 + 0.9 x$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Verify and compute the minimum cost $J^*$:** *(2 marks)*

Predictions: $h(1) = 2.2$, $h(2) = 3.1$, $h(3) = 4.0$, $h(4) = 4.9$, $h(5) = 5.8$

Residuals: $(2.2-2) = 0.2$, $(3.1-3) = 0.1$, $(4.0-5) = -1.0$, $(4.9-4) = 0.9$, $(5.8-6) = -0.2$

$$J^* = \frac{1}{10} \left[ (0.2)^2 + (0.1)^2 + (-1.0)^2 + (0.9)^2 + (-0.2)^2 \right] = \frac{1}{10} [0.04 + 0.01 + 1.0 + 0.81 + 0.04] = \frac{1.90}{10} = 0.19$$

This is the *minimum* achievable cost for this dataset.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision ŌĆö High-Density Bullet Summary**

- **Linear regression with one variable** models a *straight-line* relationship: $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$.
- The **goal** of training is to find $\theta_0, \theta_1$ that minimize the **Mean Squared Error** cost $J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$.
- The cost function for linear regression is **convex (bowl-shaped)** ŌĆö gradient descent is *guaranteed* to find the global minimum (if $\alpha$ is reasonable).
- **Gradient descent update rules (memorize both):**
  - $\theta_0 := \theta_0 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})$
  - $\theta_1 := \theta_1 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) \cdot x^{(i)}$
- **Simultaneous update** is mandatory ŌĆö never use a freshly-updated $\theta_0$ when computing the new $\theta_1$.
- **Learning rate $\alpha$:** too small ŌåÆ slow; too large ŌåÆ divergence; just right ŌåÆ smooth convergence.
- **Closed-form (Normal equation)** for one variable:
  - $\theta_1 = \frac{\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})}{\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^2}$
  - $\theta_0 = \bar{y} - \theta_1 \bar{x}$
- **Vectorized normal equation:** $\boldsymbol{\theta} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T \mathbf{y}$ ŌĆö useful when $n$ is small (no feature scaling needed).
- **Convergence check:** stop when $\vert \Delta J \vert < \varepsilon$ or gradient norm $< \varepsilon$ or max iterations reached.
- **Feature scaling** (mean-normalization, $x := \frac{x - \bar{x}}{\sigma}$) speeds up gradient descent but is *not needed* for the normal equation.
- **Worked-example protocol** for KTU numericals: (1) state $m$, (2) compute errors, (3) update $\theta_0, \theta_1$ simultaneously, (4) compute new $J$, (5) verify $J$ decreased.
- **Common exam mistakes to avoid:** forgetting $\frac{1}{m}$ factor; sequential updates; omitting the chain-rule step; not verifying that $J$ is decreasing across iterations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Regression with Multiple Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT632 - PECNT632
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Linear Regression with Multiple Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiple Linear Regression (MLR)** is a supervised machine learning algorithm that models the linear relationship between **two or more independent (input) variables** and a **single continuous dependent (output) variable** by fitting a hyperplane of best fit through the multidimensional feature space.

Mathematically, for $n$ input features, the hypothesis is:

$$h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \cdots + \theta_n x_n$$

where:
- $x_j$ = the $j^{th}$ feature (independent variable)
- $\theta_j$ = the $j^{th}$ parameter (weight/coefficient) to be learned
- $\theta_0$ = the **bias** (intercept) term
- $m$ = number of training samples
- $n$ = number of features

### 1.2 Standard KTU Notation

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $m$ | Number of training examples | $10 \le m \le 10^6$ |
| $n$ | Number of features | $1 \le n \le 10^4$ |
| $x^{(i)}$ | Feature vector of $i^{th}$ sample | $\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ |
| $y^{(i)}$ | Target value of $i^{th}$ sample | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| $\theta$ | Parameter vector | $\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ |
| $\alpha$ | Learning rate (hyperparameter) | $10^{-4} \le \alpha \le 1$ |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The House Price Crystal Ball"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition (Intuitive Overview)**
>
> Imagine you are a real estate analyst in Kerala trying to predict the **price of a house** in Kochi. In simple (univariate) regression, you would only look at one factor ŌĆö say the **size in square feet**. But in reality, house price depends on **many factors simultaneously**:
>
> 1. Size of the house ($x_1$)
> 2. Number of bedrooms ($x_2$)
> 3. Age of the building in years ($x_3$)
> 4. Distance from the city center in km ($x_4$)
>
> **Multiple Linear Regression** is like solving a multi-factor puzzle: it finds the *one straight hyperplane* (a plane in 3D, a hyperplane in higher dimensions) that best fits all the data points in the $(n+1)$-dimensional space. Each parameter $\theta_j$ tells us **"how much the house price changes when we increase feature $x_j$ by one unit, while keeping all other features constant."**
>
> Just as a good recipe balances salt, sugar, and spice, the algorithm balances multiple features to minimize the overall prediction error.

## 1.4 Why Move from One Variable to Many?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In real engineering and scientific problems, a single input variable almost never determines the output alone. For example:
> - **Civil Engineering:** Concrete strength depends on cement, water, sand, aggregate ratios.
> - **Mechanical Engineering:** Engine fuel efficiency depends on RPM, load, temperature, air-fuel ratio.
> - **Electronics:** Power dissipation in a transistor depends on voltage, current, and temperature.
>
> Multiple Linear Regression captures these **multi-factor dependencies** simultaneously, providing a more accurate and realistic model.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hypothesis Representation

The hypothesis for multiple variables in **vectorized form** is:

$$h_{\theta}(x) = \theta^{T} x$$

where $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{(n+1) \times 1}$ and $x_0 = 1$ is the bias feature.

### Why Vectorization Matters in KTU Exams
> [!NOTE]
> Examiners expect students to write the **matrix form** of the hypothesis because it is computationally efficient and forms the basis of the **Normal Equation derivation**. Always include $x_0 = 1$ to make the bias term $\theta_0$ work uniformly.

## 2.2 The Cost Function (Mean Squared Error)

The cost function measures **how wrong** the model's predictions are on average:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^{2}$$

In vectorized form:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} (X\theta - y)^{T} (X\theta - y)$$

where $X$ is the $m \times (n+1)$ design matrix and $y$ is the $m \times 1$ target vector.

## 2.3 Gradient Descent Algorithm

The update rule for **simultaneous** update of all parameters:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} J(\theta)$$

After taking the partial derivative (shown in Section 3), we obtain:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

**Critical: Simultaneous Update Rule**

$$\begin{aligned}
temp_0 &:= \theta_0 - \alpha \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) \\
temp_1 &:= \theta_1 - \alpha \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) \cdot x_1^{(i)} \\
temp_2 &:= \theta_2 - \alpha \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) \cdot x_2^{(i)} \\
\vdots & \\
\theta_j &:= temp_j \quad \text{for all } j
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Always compute all $temp_j$ values first using the **old** $\theta$ values, then assign them to $\theta_j$. Updating $\theta_0$ before computing $temp_1$ will lead to wrong answers ŌĆö examiners deduct 2 marks for this.

## 2.4 Feature Scaling & Mean Normalization

When features have vastly different scales, gradient descent takes a **highly elongated zigzag path** toward the minimum, making convergence slow. We rescale features:

$$x_j := \frac{x_j - \mu_j}{s_j}$$

where:
- $\mu_j$ = mean of feature $j$ across all training samples
- $s_j$ = range (max - min) or standard deviation of feature $j$

After scaling, every feature ideally lies in the range $\left[ -1, 1 \right]$ or $\left[ -3, 3 \right]$.

## 2.5 Learning Rate Selection

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Recommended Values:** Try $\alpha \in \left\{ 0.001,\ 0.003,\ 0.01,\ 0.03,\ 0.1,\ 0.3,\ 1 \right\}$ ŌĆö increasing by roughly 3├Ś each time. Plot $J(\theta)$ vs. iterations to diagnose convergence.

| Symptom Observed | Diagnosis | Remedy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $J(\theta)$ decreases steadily | Good convergence | Keep $\alpha$ |
| $J(\theta)$ increases or oscillates | $\alpha$ too large | Try smaller $\alpha$ (e.g., divide by 3) |
| $J(\theta)$ decreases very slowly | $\alpha$ too small | Try larger $\alpha$ |
| $J(\theta)$ plateaus quickly | Local minimum or converged | Adjust model complexity |

## 2.6 Polynomial Regression (Brief)

By creating new features like $x_1^2, x_1^3, x_1 x_2$, etc., linear regression can fit **non-linear** curves. For example:

$$h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 (\text{size}) + \theta_2 (\text{size})^2 + \theta_3 (\text{size})^3$$

## 2.7 Normal Equation ŌĆö The Closed-Form Solution

Instead of iterating, we can solve for $\theta$ **in one shot**:

$$\theta = (X^{T} X)^{-1} X^{T} y$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Normal Equation requires $X^T X$ to be **invertible**. If it is non-invertible (singular), causes include: redundant features, or insufficient training data ($m \le n$). Use the **pseudo-inverse** via SVD as a robust fallback.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Use Case / Note |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Hypothesis (vectorized) | $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta^{T} x$ | With $x_0 = 1$ convention |
| Cost Function | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^{2}$ | MSE divided by $2m$ |
| Cost Function (vectorized) | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}(X\theta - y)^{T}(X\theta - y)$ | Compact matrix form |
| Gradient Descent Rule | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m}(h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$ | Update **simultaneously** |
| Feature Scaling | $x_j := (x_j - \mu_j) / s_j$ | $\mu$ = mean, $s$ = range or SD |
| Normal Equation | $\theta = (X^{T} X)^{-1} X^{T} y$ | Closed-form solution |
| Design Matrix Dimension | $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times (n+1)}$ | $m$ rows, $n+1$ columns |
| Parameter Vector | $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{(n+1) \times 1}$ | Includes $\theta_0$ |
| Convergence Check | $J(\theta)$ decreases monotonically | Plot $J(\theta)$ vs iteration |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Multiple Linear Regression is the **backbone of predictive modeling** in engineering:
> - **Civil:** Predicting concrete compressive strength from mix proportions.
> - **Electrical:** Modeling power consumption from voltage, current, and ambient temperature.
> - **Industrial:** Forecasting equipment failure probability from sensor readings (vibration, temperature, pressure).
> - **Economics/Management:** Demand forecasting using price, advertisement spend, season index.
>
> It is also the **theoretical foundation** upon which more advanced algorithms like Logistic Regression, Neural Networks, and Support Vector Machines are built.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Normal Equation (Closed-Form Solution)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is one of the most frequently asked **14-mark derivations** in KTU Board Exams. Master every step below.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Start with the Vectorized Cost Function

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} (X\theta - y)^{T} (X\theta - y)$$

> Here, $X$ is the $m \times (n+1)$ design matrix (each row is $x^{(i)T}$), $\theta$ is $(n+1) \times 1$, and $y$ is $m \times 1$.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Expand the Quadratic Term

Let us denote $A = X\theta - y$, so $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} A^{T} A$.

Using the identity $A^T A = \sum_k A_k^2$ (sum of squares) and expanding:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ (X\theta)^{T}(X\theta) - (X\theta)^{T} y - y^{T}(X\theta) + y^{T} y \right]$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Use the Scalar Symmetry Identity

Since $(X\theta)^{T} y$ and $y^{T}(X\theta)$ are both $1 \times 1$ (scalars), they are equal:

$$(X\theta)^{T} y = y^{T}(X\theta)$$

Therefore we can combine them:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ \theta^{T} X^{T} X \theta - 2(X\theta)^{T} y + y^{T} y \right]$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Differentiate with Respect to $\theta$

We use two key matrix calculus identities:
1. $\nabla_{\theta}(\theta^{T} A \theta) = (A + A^{T})\theta$ ŌåÆ reduces to $2A\theta$ if $A$ is symmetric.
2. $\nabla_{\theta}(B^{T}\theta) = B$
3. $\nabla_{\theta}(c) = 0$ for constant $c$

Since $X^{T} X$ is **symmetric** (because $(X^{T}X)^{T} = X^{T}(X^{T})^{T} = X^{T} X$):

$$\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ 2 X^{T} X \theta - 2 X^{T} y \right]$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Simplify the Gradient Expression

$$\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta) = \frac{1}{m} X^{T} (X\theta - y)$$

> The factor of $2$ cancels with $2$ in the denominator, leaving the clean expression above.

### Step 6 ŌĆö Set the Gradient to Zero (Optimality Condition)

To find the minimum, we set $\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta) = 0$:

$$\frac{1}{m} X^{T} (X\theta - y) = 0$$

Multiplying both sides by $m$ (nonzero):

$$X^{T} (X\theta - y) = 0$$

### Step 7 ŌĆö Distribute $X^{T}$ and Solve

$$X^{T} X \theta - X^{T} y = 0$$

$$X^{T} X \theta = X^{T} y$$

### Step 8 ŌĆö Final Closed-Form Solution

Multiplying both sides by $(X^{T}X)^{-1}$ (assuming $X^T X$ is invertible):

$$\boxed{\theta = (X^{T} X)^{-1} X^{T} y}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** This entire derivation is worth **7 marks** when it appears as a sub-question. Always label each step clearly. Examiners look for the **scalar symmetry simplification** in Step 3 and the **symmetry property of $X^T X$** in Step 4 ŌĆö these are common step-skipping points where students lose marks.

## 3.2 Derivation of Gradient Descent Update Rule

### Step 1 ŌĆö Start with the Definition of Gradient Descent

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} J(\theta)$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute the Cost Function

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^{2}$$

Take the partial derivative:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} 2 \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Differentiate the Hypothesis

Recall $h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1^{(i)} + \theta_2 x_2^{(i)} + \cdots + \theta_n x_n^{(i)}$

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) = x_j^{(i)}$$

> Since $y^{(i)}$ is a constant with respect to $\theta_j$, its derivative is zero.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute Back

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} J(\theta) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Final Update Rule

$$\boxed{\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö One Step of Gradient Descent

> [!NOTE]
> This **3-mark calculation-style question** appears frequently in KTU exams. Practice it thoroughly.

**Given Data (m = 2 samples, n = 2 features):**

| Sample $i$ | $x_1^{(i)}$ | $x_2^{(i)}$ | $y^{(i)}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 1 | 2 | 5 |
| 2 | 2 | 3 | 7 |

**Initial Parameters:** $\theta_0 = 0, \theta_1 = 1, \theta_2 = 1$, Learning Rate: $\alpha = 0.1$

### Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the Hypothesis for Each Sample

For sample 1:
$$h_{\theta}(x^{(1)}) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1^{(1)} + \theta_2 x_2^{(1)} = 0 + 1(1) + 1(2) = 3$$

For sample 2:
$$h_{\theta}(x^{(2)}) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1^{(2)} + \theta_2 x_2^{(2)} = 0 + 1(2) + 1(3) = 5$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the Errors

$$\text{Error}_1 = h_{\theta}(x^{(1)}) - y^{(1)} = 3 - 5 = -2$$

$$\text{Error}_2 = h_{\theta}(x^{(2)}) - y^{(2)} = 5 - 7 = -2$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Compute Update for $\theta_0$

$$\begin{aligned}
temp_0 &:= \theta_0 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\text{Error}_i) \\
&= 0 - 0.1 \cdot \frac{1}{2}(-2 + (-2)) \\
&= 0 - 0.1 \cdot (-2) \\
&= 0.2
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Compute Update for $\theta_1$

$$\begin{aligned}
temp_1 &:= \theta_1 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\text{Error}_i \cdot x_1^{(i)}) \\
&= 1 - 0.1 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left( (-2)(1) + (-2)(2) \right) \\
&= 1 - 0.1 \cdot \frac{1}{2}(-6) \\
&= 1 - 0.1 \cdot (-3) \\
&= 1 + 0.3 \\
&= 1.3
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Compute Update for $\theta_2$

$$\begin{aligned}
temp_2 &:= \theta_2 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\text{Error}_i \cdot x_2^{(i)}) \\
&= 1 - 0.1 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left( (-2)(2) + (-2)(3) \right) \\
&= 1 - 0.1 \cdot \frac{1}{2}(-10) \\
&= 1 - 0.1 \cdot (-5) \\
&= 1 + 0.5 \\
&= 1.5
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Simultaneous Assignment

$$\theta_0 = 0.2, \quad \theta_1 = 1.3, \quad \theta_2 = 1.5$$

## 3.4 Convergence Analysis & Comparison

| Aspect | Gradient Descent | Normal Equation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Method | Iterative (many steps) | Closed-form (one step) |
| Choice of $\alpha$ | Required | Not needed |
| Computational cost per iteration | $O(kn)$ where $k$ = iterations | $O(n^3)$ for matrix inversion |
| Total cost for $n$ features | $O(kn m)$ | $O(n^3)$ to invert + $O(n^2 m)$ for $X^T X$ |
| Works when $n$ is large ($> 10^4$) | **Yes** | **No ŌĆö too slow** |
| Requires feature scaling | **Yes** | No |
| Handles non-invertible $X^T X$ | Yes (skips matrix) | Needs pseudo-inverse |
| Best suited for | Large datasets, online learning | Small datasets ($n < 1000$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Decision Rule:**
> - If $n < 1000$ ŌåÆ Normal Equation is fast and exact.
> - If $n > 10000$ ŌåÆ Use Gradient Descent (Normal Equation becomes prohibitively slow).
> - If $1000 \le n \le 10000$ ŌåÆ Either works; often Gradient Descent is preferred for flexibility.
